title: Garlic honey for colds
subtitle: Raw garlic macerated in honey, an old-fashioned kitchen remedy
excerpt: Crushed garlic cloves left to macerate in raw honey for 24 hours. The combination has been used for centuries as a first-response kitchen remedy at the onset of a cold. Garlic's antimicrobial and immune-stimulating properties are documented in multiple WHO monographs.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), garlic entry, antimicrobial, expectorant, and immune-stimulating properties. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), garlic as an antiseptic and cold remedy. WHO Monograph on Allium sativum (1999), evidence review of allicin as the key antimicrobial constituent.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch in a larger jar. Keeps at room temperature for 4 weeks.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make at the first hint of the cold season. The garlic needs at least 24 hours in the honey before use, 48 hours is better. It improves for the first week as the allicin infuses.

recipeTools.notes: A small sterilised jar with a tight-fitting lid.
For crushing the garlic if you don't have a press.

glossaryTerms.definition: The primary antimicrobial and sulphur-containing compound released when garlic is crushed or cut, responsible for its characteristic smell and traditional immune-stimulating action.

content.text: Crushed garlic left to macerate in raw honey for 24 hours. The honey draws out the

and other sulphur compounds from the garlic; the honey itself softens the harshness and acts as a mild preservative. Taken by the teaspoon at the onset of a cold, or stirred into a mug of hot water with lemon, this is one of those kitchen remedies that crosses cultures and centuries. Maud Grieve (1931) notes garlic's antimicrobial and expectorant properties at length; the WHO monograph on Allium sativum (1999) confirms the evidence for allicin as the key active constituent. The result is pungent, distinctive, and very effective at reminding everyone in the household that someone is trying to ward off a cold.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Anticoagulants: garlic has demonstrated antiplatelet activity in studies. At culinary doses (one to two cloves a day) this is generally insignificant. Therapeutic use, more than three cloves daily or daily concentrated garlic preparations, is best discussed with the prescriber if you are taking warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, or other anticoagulants.

Children under two: do not give honey to children under two years. Honey can carry Clostridium botulinum spores. For children aged two and over, half a teaspoon is within the traditional range.

Pregnancy: culinary amounts of garlic throughout pregnancy are well established as safe. Therapeutic-dose garlic every day is less studied in pregnancy at concentrated levels, one or two teaspoons of garlic honey daily is likely within a conservative culinary range, but discuss with your midwife if you are using it regularly.

Breath and body odour: allicin is excreted partly through the lungs and skin. This is expected and temporary.

items.prepNote: crushed or coarsely chopped, the crushing releases the allicin
raw, unpasteurised honey if possible, it retains its own antimicrobial compounds

content.text: Peel {{garlic}} and crush or coarsely chop each clove, a flat blade pressed down with the heel of the hand, or a few pulses in a pestle and mortar. The goal is to break the cell walls and release the allicin; slicing thinly achieves the same effect. Place the crushed garlic directly into the sterilised jar.

Pour {{honey}} of honey over the garlic. Stir to combine, the honey will be thick and the garlic will settle on the sides. Seal the jar. Within a few hours the garlic will begin to release liquid into the honey, making it more pourable. Leave at room temperature for at least 24 hours, ideally 48, before using. Turn the jar upside down and back periodically to distribute the garlic through the honey.

Adults: 1 teaspoon (5 ml) of the garlic honey, taken neat or stirred into a small cup of warm water with a squeeze of lemon, up to three times a day at the onset of a cold. Continue for three to five days. The garlic cloves themselves can also be eaten, one or two crushed cloves a day is within the traditional therapeutic range. If the cold is not improving after five days, see your doctor.

Keeps at room temperature in a sealed jar for 4 weeks; refrigerate for up to 3 months. The honey will crystallise in the fridge, gentle warming in a bowl of warm water returns it to a pourable consistency.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not give to children under two. Discuss with prescriber if taking anticoagulants. See your doctor if cold symptoms are accompanied by high fever (above 38.5°C), difficulty breathing, chest pain, or a severe worsening of symptoms after initial improvement, 'secondary dip' three to five days into a cold can indicate bacterial secondary infection requiring antibiotics.
